标题: Why Trump Officials and VCs Love This Nuclear Power Startup’s Brute-Force Approach
内容:
On a cloudy day in late March, Isaiah Taylor, the 27-year-old founder and chief executive of Valar Atomics, a Hawthorne, Calif.–based nuclear energy startup, led me into a large black tent at the Utah San Rafael Energy Lab in the middle of the Utah desert. Dozens of construction workers and beeping forklifts buzzed around the nearly 40-foot-tall reactor rising from the center of the tent, with an enormous American flag in the background.
Outside, Taylor gestured around to the empty landscape surrounding the site, which Valar is leasing from the state of Utah. “Six months ago, this looked like that field out there,” he said, pointing to the untouched beige ground stretching toward the mountains. “We built this in six months.”
Six months ago is around the time Valar was selected as one of 11 startups in a Department of Energy pilot program aimed at getting nuclear power startups to reach criticality—the point at which their reactors produce power through self-perpetuating chain reactions—by July 4. Carrying out orders from President Donald Trump’s White House, the agency coupled this program with a sweeping overhaul of its regulatory process and safety rules to make it easier and faster to build reactors.
Over the past two decades, dozens of startups like Valar have launched on the promise that small modular reactors, which can generate up to a third of the power of traditional nuclear plants, could make nuclear energy cheaper and faster than conventional reactors can. They hope to achieve that by building SMRs in factories, unlike the bespoke, often ruinously over-budget engineering projects many nuclear power plants amount to. Yet despite billions in investment, none of the SMR startups has successfully brought a reactor online anywhere in the world (the only operational SMRs are in Russia and China and built by state-owned enterprises).
That made the DOE’s timeline seem wildly ambitious to many in the industry. But Taylor now says Valar may switch its reactor on even sooner—possibly within weeks. “That’s a pretty audacious claim,” said Daniel Siefman, an assistant professor of nuclear engineering at the University of California, Berkeley. He noted that it “usually takes a couple of years at least” to turn on a reactor. If Valar achieves its goal, it will have done so in just over a year and a half.
Among all the companies in the DOE program—which includes more established players like publicly traded Oklo Inc. and Terrestrial Energy, a 14-year-old Charlotte, N.C., company—Valar has emerged as one of the industry’s most aggressive startups. It may also be its most polarizing.
Taylor’s grand claims and battle against a key nuclear regulator have made him a divisive figure, particularly in traditional nuclear circles. At the same time, Valar is one of the best-funded nuclear startups in Trump’s pilot program. In March, it
reportedly
raised $450 million at a $2 billion valuation in a mix of debt and equity. A number of Valar’s investors, including Anduril co-founder Palmer Luckey and Shyam Shankar, Palantir Technologies’ chief technology officer, have ties to the Trump administration, with whom Valar has also become deeply engrained.
Steve Marcus—one of Valar’s investors and cofounder of Los Angeles–based Riot Ventures—argues that Taylor himself has helped drive some of the changes happening in the administration’s approach to nuclear power. “This administration, they listen to Valar above all about what strategy they need to put into place,” said Marcus, who is on Valar’s board.
Investors are pouring a fortune into nuclear energy startups, believing they could be the solution to soaring energy demand from data centers, electric vehicles and other energy-intensive technologies. Data centers in particular are rapidly proliferating across the country as demand for AI computing accelerates, driving an unprecedented demand for power.
In response, major tech companies are scrambling to secure every conceivable energy source they can, including nuclear, which some view as one of the most attractive sources of carbon-free electricity. Like many nuclear startups, Valar hopes to mass-produce small reactors that could eventually power industrial facilities and data centers through nuclear campuses, or gigasites.
Valar’s reactor in Utah. Courtesy of Valar Atomics
One investor who decided not to back Valar told me it’s “hilarious” how controversial Taylor is among nuclear experts, fellow investors and entrepreneurs, and added that he attracts detractors and fans in equal parts. Nuclear scientists told me Valar is particularly good at self-promotion in a field rife with hype, where most entrants have not shown clear plans for how they’ll actually pull off their ambitious goals.
“Maybe they’ll do well, and it’s just ‘Fake it till you make it,’” Siefman said. “But I’m like, a lot of money is going to get lost…in these companies where they don’t have a product and they’re figuring out down the line what the product is.”
A high school dropout with no formal experience in nuclear engineering, Taylor founded Valar three years ago after becoming convinced that he could help fix an industry long mired in regulatory, cost and safety constraints.
“Why do I care about this?” Taylor, a devout Christian and father of four, wrote in a 2023
post on X
announcing the company. “Because I believe that God created the world to be abundant. Because cheap energy is the best lever I have to unleash the inflection of technological blessings, which have been held back from us since the ’70s.”
In many ways, Taylor is the type of entrepreneur who perfectly embodies today’s tech CEO zeitgeist. He fits in with the industry’s rightward shift and the growing disdain for college degrees and credentialism among figures like Peter Thiel, Palantir’s Alex Karp and others. In this world, founders are getting younger and hungrier and are omnipresent online.
On X, Taylor often posts videos of Valar’s reactor under construction, edited like “Mission Impossible” movies. At Valar’s site in Utah, I learned that he had hired Darren Doane—a filmmaker who previously followed the band Blink-182 around on tour—to make even more videos.
Increasingly, a startup founder’s self-confidence is what matters most to investors—and Taylor has that quality in abundance.
“The fact that he was actually a self-trained nuclear physicist or whatever you want to call it, that actually appealed to me,” said Valar investor Doug Philippone, who spent 16 years running Palantir’s defense business before founding his Palm Beach, Fla.–based venture firm, Snowpoint Ventures, in 2021. “Like, only in America can that kind of stuff happen.”
At times, though, Taylor’s bold claims on social media have ignited heated arguments with nuclear experts.
Valar’s reactor aboard an Air Force C-17 cargo plane. Courtesy of Valar Atomics
In February, for example, Valar announced it was partnering with the DOE and the Department of Defense for a military operation, in which a trio of Air Force C-17 cargo planes transported the company’s reactor (without its fuel and concrete shielding) from California to Utah. On X, Taylor described it as “
the first C-17 airlift of a nuclear reactor
.” Once the parts arrived in Utah, Taylor stood with Utah Gov. Spencer Cox and DOE Secretary Chris Wright, who
claimed it was the first
moving of a nuclear reactor by air.
But military planes have transported reactors many times before, said Nick Touran, a nuclear engineer who previously worked at Valar competitors Aalo Atomics and TerraPower and who at one point
sparred
with Taylor over another of his claims. “It’s not meaningless, like it’s good for the military to practice tying stuff down like that,” said Touran, though he largely characterized the event as smart publicity and “cool imagery.”
Todd Allen, a nuclear engineering professor at the University of Michigan, was more blunt: “When you’re constantly bragging about stuff that’s not a big deal, it’s like, is there a
there
there?”
Rian Bahran, the DOE’s deputy assistant secretary for nuclear reactors, said Valar paid to use the aircraft. Bahran suggested other companies could have made similar arrangements but instead opted to drive their reactors to sites. It likely cost Valar hundreds of thousands of dollars, but the exercise seems to have paid off in terms of attracting attention: Dozens of news outlets covered the launch.
Taylor conceded that the event was a small milestone and shrugged off the criticisms as reflective of a broken industry. “Unfortunately, the traditional nuclear industry is more impressed by models and simulations and LOIs and MOUs than by actual hardware milestones,” he said. “For Valar, it’s just the opposite.”
Taylor’s path into the nuclear industry was an unconventional one. Born in Normal, Ill., a city of about 50,000 people north of Springfield, he has described a childhood in which he grew up on food stamps and frequently moved around the country as his father took on various software consulting and marketing jobs.
When Taylor was 12, his father bought him a computer on the condition that he find a way to pay for college. Taylor taught himself to code and then dropped out of high school at 16 when he got a job as a software engineer at PDPglobal, a Colorado Springs, Colo.–based human resources software company.
He used the job’s six-figure salary to launch Erber Auto, an auto repair shop in Moscow, Idaho, in 2016, before pursuing another automotive business with his father, HoodHub, three years later.
After that, Taylor did software engineering projects for the DOD and hedge fund Bridgewater Associates, he has said. Neither Bridgewater nor the DOD responded to inquiries about Taylor’s work for them. When I asked him for more details on his roles at both, he said, “A lot of my work there’s under NDA.”
Isaiah Taylor, CEO of Valar Atomics. Photo by Tasos Katopodis/Getty Images
Taylor had his eyes on a bigger ambition, though: starting a nuclear business. His passion for nuclear energy stemmed from the legacy of his great-grandfather, Ward Schaap, who worked on the Manhattan Project as a scientist in the 1940s. Schaap was the inspiration for the name of Valar’s first reactor, the Ward 250.
When Taylor launched Valar in 2023, investors were skeptical about its prospects. Taylor said he pitched more than 60 venture capital firms before eventually securing a $1.5 million investment from Marcus of Riot Ventures. Taylor’s lack of credentials didn’t faze Marcus. “If you’re going to build a company, the most successful attribute is really leadership, and leadership is being able to tell a story, communicate a vision,” Marcus said.
In early 2024, Valar hired its first nuclear engineer: Fakhrul Islam, a recent  graduate of the University of South Carolina with a nuclear engineering doctorate, and then brought on industry veteran Mark Mitchell to lead reactor development.
It has also hired employees from other industries. Valar’s president, Muhammad Shahzad, is the former chief financial officer of Relativity Space, a rocket ship startup recently taken over by billionaire Eric Schmidt. Its former director of engineering, Carl Lumma, previously worked on MacBooks at Apple.
Shahzad wears his newcomer status proudly. “I’m new to nuclear, new to energy,” he emphasized multiple times in an interview.
At Valar’s Utah site, I met Sean Stassi, a 21-year-old mechanical engineer overseeing the design of the concrete shielding around the reactor, a critical safety feature meant to prevent radiation leaks. Stassi met Taylor at a bonfire party on the beach in El Segundo, Calif., two years ago and decided to take a break from studying at El Camino College in LA, where he’d just finished his sophomore year.
I asked him if he planned to return to school. “We’ll see what happens,” he said. “Depends on how this whole thing goes.”
Though widely viewed as cleaner and safer than many other energy sources, nuclear power has long faced a reputational problem in the U.S., exacerbated by soaring construction costs and lingering fears sparked by disasters like Chernobyl and Fukushima. In the U.S., the Three Mile Island accident in Pennsylvania in 1979 effectively shut down construction of new nuclear power plants for several decades.
Beginning in the early 2000s, a wave of startups—such as NuScale Power, X-energy and TerraPower, which Bill Gates co-founded—argued that smaller reactors could make nuclear cheaper and easier to deploy. These companies largely followed the traditional path of filing the plans for their reactors through the Nuclear Regulatory Commission, the government agency set up in 1975 to serve as an independent regulator of new nuclear projects.
A few years ago, a new generation of startups emerged, including Valar, Last Energy and Deep Fission. They brought with them what Siefman, the nuclear engineering professor, described as a distinctly Silicon Valley mentality, marked by “aggressive promises” and a drive to “push and push and push.”
This newer generation wants to see the federal government strip down nuclear regulations. Taylor argued that one of the core barriers to building more nuclear power sources has been the government approval process. He believes companies should be allowed to test prototypes before spending years navigating commerical licensing requirements.
“Imagine if you wanted to develop a new car, but you could never test drive it until it was fully certified to sell to a million people,” he said.
Last April, Valar joined Last Energy and Deep Fission, along with Texas, Utah, Florida and Louisiana, in a lawsuit against the NRC. The ongoing lawsuit argued that the regulator shouldn’t employ the same level of scrutiny with smaller reactors that it has used to assess large nuclear plants in the past.
In May 2025, President Trump signed executive orders directing the NRC to accelerate reactor approvals and overhaul its existing regulatory frameworks.  And while the agency historically was the only group authorizing certain commercial reactors, Trump has involved the DOE in the process as well.
Since then, the DOE has rewritten key safety and security requirements, including some related to radiation exposure, water protections and physical security standards at nuclear sites.
“We removed redundancies,” said Bahran, the DOE deputy director. “Safety continues to be the highest priority, and we’re not going to authorize startup reactors we’re not confident are safe to operate.”
The nuclear experts I spoke with didn’t disagree with the idea that the NRC’s licensing process could benefit from modernization. But they argued that criticisms of the agency by startups like Valar are overblown and that the strict protocols were there to protect people.
“This rhetoric about the NRC and the difficulty of getting something licensed, some of these companies took it and ran with it,” said Scott Morris, who was the NRC’s deputy director for operations until last June.
“Is it a rigorous licensing process? Absolutely,” Morris continued. “They’re going to do a comprehensive deep dive into the risk and the safety aspects of your design to make sure that once a license is issued, the damn thing is going to be safe.”
Even if Valar manages to switch on its reactor before Independence Day, the company could still face a long and thorny road to commercialization, one that includes approval from the NRC (unlike most of its peers in the DOE program, Valar has
yet to file any
paperwork
with the agency) and questions about how exactly the startup will turn a profit.
To make the July deadline, Valar has focused on carefully managing expenses, said Shahzad, its president. “Crassly put, we’re trying to make the Toyota Camry of reactors,” he said. “I would guess—and I have no detailed insight on this—that on a per kilowatt basis, ours is the cheapest reactor.”
Instead of waiting around for expensive materials, Shahzad said the team’s first question is always: “Can’t you just get it across the street at Home Depot?” After figuring out how to build a reactor cheaply, the company will focus on improving efficiency over time.
Workers atop Valar’s reactor in Utah. Courtesy of Valar Atomics
But Valar’s commercial strategy is still fuzzy. For example, it’s not clear where it will eventually mass-produce its reactors. Shahzad said, “Where we’re making what is TBD.” Taylor indicated that manufacturing will remain in LA for the near term. And while competitors like Oklo and TerraPower
have inked deals with tech giants
to eventually supply electricity for data centers, Valar has announced no such deals.
“We think that’s kind of putting the cart before the horse to announce a bunch of big partnerships before you actually turn a reactor on,” Taylor said.
Getting a project to go critical is one thing, said Jigar Shah, who until early last year was director of the DOE’s loan programs office under President Joe Biden, responsible for financing clean-energy projects. But “that doesn’t mean you’re going to cost-effectively produce a plant that works.”
Shah also warned that the favorable political climate that’s lubricating progress for Valar and other nuclear startups may shift. Valar could still be years away from a commercially viable reactor. “Valar might be able to cut that in half and do it in three and a half years, but that still puts you after the Trump administration leaves office,” he said.
